title: Chapter 27: The Market Reacts
content: Word traveled fast in the Nether-Grid.

By sunrise, every entity in the Exchange knew what had happened at Blackwood. A human woman — a mortal — had bought out an entire zone's worth of debt contracts. She had turned a Frost-bringer into an employee. She had made the Senior Examiner retreat with his ledgers in ash.
The Exchange did not panic. The Exchange was not capable of panic. But it did something worse.
It adjusted.
"The Obol is devaluing."
Frost stood in Roxanne's office at six in the morning. Its crystalline fingers were tracing symbols in the air — floating numbers that shifted and flickered as it spoke.
"By how much?"

"Locally? 23 percent since midnight. The Exchange is flooding the market with counterfeit Obols within your territory. They cannot invalidate your card, but they can destabilize the currency you operate in."
Roxanne looked at the floating numbers. "So they're printing fake money to mess with my economy."
"Accurate."
"That's... actually kind of clever."
"It is a standard tactic. When a superior force cannot be defeated directly, they corrupt the environment in which it operates."
"So what do I do?"
Frost's hood tilted. "Historically, territorial governors in your position have responded by —"
"No. I'm not asking what other people did. I'm asking what I should do."
Silence.
"...You wish to innovate."
"I wish to not play their game."

Roxanne stood up. She walked to the window of the dean's office, looking out at the campus below. Refugees were lining up at the dining hall. Children were playing in what used to be the main quad. The Executioner stood at the gate, motionless, a silent promise of safety.
She had built this. Seven thousand people depended on it.
And the Exchange was trying to burn it down with monopoly money.
"If I can't stop them from printing fake Obols..."
She turned back to Frost.
"What if I make Obols irrelevant?"
Frost's crystalline fingers paused mid-calculation.
"Explain."

"The Obol has value because the Exchange says it has value. It's backed by nothing except the threat of enforcement. But my card —" she held it up "— is backed by something real. The original contract. The Void bloodline. An infinite balance."
"Correct."

"So what if I issue my own currency? Backed by the card. One-to-one redeemable. People use my money instead of Obols, and suddenly the Exchange's counterfeit operation doesn't matter because nobody is using Obols anymore."
Frost was silent for a long time.
Then it said: "That is not how currency has ever worked in the Nether-Grid."
"Good. I don't want to do things the way they've always worked."
"...You would need a minting authority."
"You're my head of accounting. You're a Frost-bringer. You literally defined what debt means. Can you mint currency?"
Another long silence.
"It is... not outside my capabilities."
"Great. Let's make money."
Frost raised a crystalline hand.
"There are practical considerations. A currency requires trust. The refugees trust you, but the wider Nether-Grid does not. Your money would only be accepted within your territory."
"For now."
"For now. And the Exchange will not allow this to go unchallenged. Economic secession is a declaration of war."
Roxanne smiled.
"Good. I've been wanting to declare war."
Three days later, the first Void Mark notes were issued.

Frost had designed them. Each note was a palm-sized rectangle of something that wasn't quite paper — it had the weight of silk and the texture of frozen glass. In the center, a black card emblem shimmered with the ∞ symbol. The edges were lined with Frost's crystalline signature — geometric patterns that shifted when viewed from different angles.
Roxanne held the first note.
"What do I call this?"
"It is a promissory note backed by the Primordial Black Card. Technically, it is a bearer bond denominated in —"
"No. I need a name. Something that sounds cool."
Frost considered. "The Void Standard?"
"Too formal."
"Shadow Script?"
"Too edgy."
"...What would you call it?"
Roxanne looked at the note. The ∞ symbol pulsed gently in the light.
"Infinity Notes."
"That is —"
"Cool? I know."
"I was going to say imprecise. But cool works too."
Lena found Roxanne in the dining hall that evening, handing out stacks of the new notes to the refugees.
"What are those?"
"Money. Our money."
Lena picked one up. The ∞ symbol caught the violet light and reflected it back in a smooth, silver shimmer.
"This is gorgeous. Who designed it?"
"Frost."
"The ice ghost?"
"The Frost-bringer. He's our head of accounting now."

Lena turned the note over. The reverse side had a simple inscription: *Redeemable at the Vance Demesne for one standard ration unit or equivalent value in goods and services. Backed by the Primordial Black Card. ∞*
"You're printing your own money."
"I'm offering a better alternative to the Obol."
"In the middle of an apocalypse."
"That's exactly when new currencies emerge. The old ones always collapse first."
Lena looked at the queue of refugees accepting the notes. They were hesitant at first. But when the first few used them at the dining hall to get food — real food, hot food — the hesitation faded.
"They trust you," Lena said quietly.
"I know."
"That's a lot of weight."
Roxanne looked at the ∞ note in her hand.
"It's a lot of weight. But it's mine."
A week passed. The Infinity Notes spread.

It started with the dining hall. Then the medical clinic. Then the refugee families began trading them among themselves — one note for a bundle of firewood, two notes for a spare blanket, half a note for a handful of dried herbs from someone's garden.
Frost tracked every transaction. Its crystalline fingers moved constantly, tracing the flow of value through the Vance Demesne.
"Adoption rate is 64 percent within the territory," it reported. "At current trajectory, Obol usage will drop below 10 percent within two weeks."
"And outside the territory?"
"Zero. The Exchange has instructed all external vendors not to accept your notes."
"They'll change their minds eventually."
"Why?"

"Because Obols are backed by threats. Infinity Notes are backed by food, shelter, and medicine. When people have to choose between a coin that might get them killed and a note that buys them dinner, they'll pick dinner every time."
Frost was silent.
Then: "Your understanding of economics is... unusually practical for someone who studied art."
"Economics is just aesthetics for numbers. I've always had an eye for composition."
That night, Silas found her in the art studio.
She was sketching. Not a design for a new note, but a portrait. Lena, laughing, with charcoal dust on her face.
"You should be resting," he said.
"I should be doing a lot of things."
"The Exchange sent another delegation today. They didn't come to campus. They went to the settlements outside your territory. Offering Obol loans to anyone who would take them."
"Predatory lending. Classic."
"They're trying to create debtors who will owe them more than they owe you."
Roxanne didn't look up from her sketch.
"Let them."
"Let them?"

"Every person who takes a loan from the Exchange is a person who will eventually need an alternative. When the interest compounds and they can't pay, they'll come here. And we'll buy their debt. Just like I did with the campus."
She added a final stroke to Lena's hair.
"The Exchange is so used to being the only option that they've forgotten what competition feels like."
Silas watched her draw.
"You're building a new economy."
"I'm building a new system. The economy is just the engine."
"And when the engine is running?"
She set down her charcoal. Looked at the portrait.
"Then I'll build something beautiful with it."
